body_txt: As long as it is still the same basic story and keeps all the best bits and characters intact, then it doesn't matter too much that Bob's bald , Alice dies in a train wreck instead of a car crash , the football game ended with a different score, and they cut the watermelon scene, right? It's a bit of a shame they screwed that bit up, but really, it's not like the entire work is Ruined FOREVER , right? WRONG! ...or so you would be told by many fans. For some people, the very act of adaptation is bad. A film version of something should be a direct word-for-word transcription , with utmost care that the sets, costumes and people be reproduced in every detail. If a character who wore a homburg in the original now wears a fedora, that will be enough to ruin the character, and therefore ruin the film. It will be all you will hear about from these fans on message boards, with them going on at length to explain how their homburg visually defined their entire personality in a way that a fedora never could, and don't you dare suggest that in changing it they made it better. If certain fans take the Chicken Little approach and announce that the sky is falling, it's mainly an expression of their fears that the writing staff don't care much about the source material, particularly if it's not mainstream-friendly . Even if the changes were enforced by Executive Meddling , the possibility of Special Effects Failure , budget constraints etc, it won't make any difference to these people; they'll still insist that any change from the original is automatically for the worse. This also happens a lot with translations. People can become religiously attached (sometimes quite literally, in the case of texts like The Bible ) to one translation of a work, and when a new translation comes out they condemn it as a travesty, accusing it of distorting and cheapening the meaning of the original, whether or not the new translation is a more literally accurate rendering. If the changes were introduced by someone other than the original creator, fans may go on at length about how Only the Creator Does It Right and any attempt to deviate from the creator's original vision is bad by definition. In cases where the creator is deceased, this may lead to cries of "They must be spinning in their grave!" Changes made due to Values Dissonance and/or to increase the diversity of the cast can also provoke complaints from some quarters. If, for example, a work includes a character who's a member of a minority, be they disabled, LGBT, a member of an ethnic minority etc, but that character is portrayed in a way which would be considered offensive today, giving the character a Race Lift or otherwise changing them so that they were not a member of that minority could lead to accusations of overcorrection and erasure. And minority actors cast in roles usually associated with non-minorities may be labelled "diversity hires" even if they play those roles well. Sometimes, the complaints are about a sequel or remake to an adaptation, and the original adaptation actually made changes from its source material, and the new adaptation tries to be more faithful , but people don't know that and complain about changes anyway. Original works can also be affected. If the work lasts long enough, fans may become attached to the characters and set-up that were introduced in the first installment to the point where changes to either lead to complaints. And if the changes involve writing a character out and replacing them with another, the new character may be labelled a Replacement Scrappy and bashed for not being their predecessor. A common complaint people have about changes to long-running TV shows is that the creators are trying too hard to keep the show "fresh" or are otherwise running out of ideas. This is sometimes a result of Ability Over Appearance . If the source describes a character as having a certain appearance, fans may expect the actor cast to play them in an adaptation to match that description as closely as possible, even if that actor is not the best choice for the role. Should the casting director go with an actor who doesn't match the character's original description, this may lead to fans refusing to accept that actor in the role and declaring that they will boycott the adaptation unless the role is recast immediately with an actor who actually looks like the character. All this being said, there are many cases wherein the fans' complaints are coming from a more solid foundation:  Compare with Translation Style Choices , Darker and Edgier , Lighter and Softer , New Sound Album , Replacement Scrappy , Ruined FOREVER , Seasonal Rot , Network Decay , Magazine Decay , Jumping the Shark , Fanon Discontinuity , They Don't Make Them Like They Used To , So Bad, It Was Better , or even Damn You, Muscle Memory! . See also Unpleasable Fanbase and Broken Base . Contrast Woolseyism , It's the Same, Now It Sucks! , Franchise Original Sin . Remember that Examples Are Not Recent , even if you're writing up an example while fan controversy is erupting. A No Recent Examples, Please! rule applies to all examples of this trope , which shouldn't be added for at least one week after release for standalone works, or the end of the season (or three months, if no season breaks) for episodic works.
